sentences: To reduce global warming, the nations of the world will have to act together.
In the meantime, individuals can also help.
Don't throw things away, recycle paper, glass, plastic, etc. so that it can be used again.
Save energy, for example switch off lights when you leave a room.
Don't waste water, for example turn off the tap when you are brushing your teeth.
Change light bulbs for more energy-efficient ones.
Drive less and walk more.
Plant trees because trees reduce the amount of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere, and so reduce greenhouse gases.
To stop global warming from destroying our environment, we need to act now.

tracks.title: Unit 59A - The job of the army
sentences: These men are soldiers, and they are carrying guns.
They are members of an army, and part of the responsibility of an army is to defend their country from attack and to protect the people.
Sometimes this means they have to fight the enemy.
A long period of fighting is called a war, for example the First World War, 1914 to 1918, and during a war there will be a number of battles.
Violence is when someone tries to hurt or kill someone.
The adjective is violent.
The violence has increased in recent weeks.
There have been a number of violent attacks.

tracks.title: Unit 59B - Reporting in war
sentences: Ten people were killed and many more were injured when a bomb exploded in the heart of the city.
The attack happened just before midday, and destroyed several buildings.
The army are now searching the area where the attack took place.
Most of the attackers escaped, but the army believe they may still be hiding in other parts of the city.
During the attack, one soldier was shot and later died.
It also appears that another soldier is missing.
The number of deaths is expected to rise.
Several words in the unit can be used as verbs and nouns with a similar meaning.
They're searching for them.
They're continuing their search for them.
He can't escape.
There is no escape.
When did they attack?
Where did the attack take place?

tracks.title: Unit 60A - Phrases with time
sentences: We got to the meeting in time.
My brother is always on time.
It's time for us to go.
Do you have time for a cup of coffee?
Call me the next time you're in London.
The weather was wonderful the last time we were there.
Sophie and I arrived at the same time, 9 o'clock exactly.

tracks.title: Unit 60B - Time prepositions often confused
sentences: I'll be here until 4.30.
I'll be there by 8.15.
I've worked here for six months.
I've worked here since May.
I worked on a farm during the summer.
I worked on a farm for a month.
I'm going back to Brazil in ten days' time.

tracks.title: Unit 60C - Approximate periods of time - past, present and future
sentences: I've known Lucia for ages.
I haven't been to the dentist recently or lately.
I saw Tom recently.
I used to go skiing, but that was a long time ago.
My sister went to the zoo the other day.
I don't see my brother much these days.
This dictionary's fine for the time being.
I'm sure I'll go to America one day.

tracks.title: Unit 60D - Counting time
sentences: There are 60 seconds in a minute.
There are 60 minutes in an hour.
There are 24 hours in a day.
There are 7 days in a week.
There are 2 weeks in a fortnight.
There are 52 weeks in a year.
There are 10 years in a decade.
There are 100 years in a century.

tracks.title: Unit 60E - Take and last
sentences: We use take to say how long we need to do something.
It takes me half an hour to get to school.
We can walk, but it'll take us a long time.
We use last to talk about how long something continues, from the beginning to the end.
The course lasts for ten weeks.
How long does the film last?
The battery in my camera didn't last long.

tracks.title: Unit 61A - Cardinal numbers
sentences: Three hundred and seventy-nine.
Two thousand, eight hundred and sixty.
Five thousand and eighty-four.
Four hundred and seventy thousand.
Two million.
Three billion.
There is no plural s after hundred, thousand, million and billion when they are part of a number.
When we are talking generally, they are plural, for example thousands of people, millions of insects.

tracks.title: Unit 61B - Dates
sentences: With dates, we write them and say them in a different way.
We can write 4 June or June 4th, but say the fourth of June or June the fourth.
We can write 21 May or May 21st, but say the twenty-first of May or May the twenty-first.
Nineteen ninety-seven.
Two thousand and sixteen, or twenty sixteen.

tracks.title: Unit 61C - Fractions and decimals
sentences: One and a quarter.
One point two five.
One and a half.
One point five.
One and three quarters.
One point seven five.

tracks.title: Unit 61D - Percentages
sentences: 26% is spoken as twenty-six per cent.
More than 50% of something is the majority of it, less than 50% of something is the minority.
The vast majority of the students, for example 95%, agreed with the new plan, only a small minority, for example 5%, were unhappy.

tracks.title: Unit 61E - Calculations
sentences: There are four basic processes.
Notice how they are said when we are working out the answer.
Six plus four equals ten.
Six minus four is two.
Six multiplied by four is twenty-four.
Eight divided by two is four.
Some people are not very good at adding up numbers, and often get stuck if they have to work out something quite difficult.
The easiest way is to use a calculator.

tracks.title: Unit 61F - Saying '0'
sentences: Zero can be spoken in different ways in different situations.
For a telephone number, 603449 is six oh three, double four nine, or six zero three, double four nine.
In mathematics, 0.7 is nought point seven, and 6.02 is six point oh two or six point nought two.
For temperature, minus 10 degrees is ten degrees below zero, or minus ten degrees.

tracks.title: Unit 62A - Distance: how far
sentences: British people buy most things in metres, centimetres and millimetres, but they still often talk about distance using the old system of miles, yards, feet, and inches.
One mile is about 1.6 kilometres.
One yard is almost 1 metre.
One foot is 30 centimetres.
One inch is 2.5 centimetres.
How far's the post office?
About a mile away.
The nearest one is half a mile.
About ten minutes' walk.
At least a couple of miles.
About a hundred yards up or down the road.
It's just round the corner.
Is it far?
Yes, it's quite a long way.

tracks.title: Unit 62B - Dimensions: how long or wide
sentences: We can ask about the dimensions like this.
What's the length of the garage?
How long is the garage?
It's five metres long.
What's the width of the path?
How wide is the path?
It's a metre wide.
What's the height of the wall?
How high is the wall?
It's two metres high.
What's the depth of the pool?
How deep is the pool?
It's three metres deep.

tracks.title: Unit 62C - Size: how big or small
sentences: We use a range of adjectives to describe the size of something.
It was a very thick book, over 500 pages.
Their living room is huge.
We caught a giant crab.
It was a very narrow road for a bus.

tracks.title: Unit 63A - Objects and materials
sentences: A bell, metal, used in hotel receptions.
A chair, wood, an antique.
A hat, fur, made from a real animal.
A bone, rubber, a toy for dogs.
A flag, cloth, the Italian national flag.
A shirt, cotton, it has red stripes.
A top, silk, beautifully soft and stylish.
A sofa, leather, stylish.
A ladder, metal, light and easy to carry.
A tissue, paper, a soft piece of paper that you use for cleaning your nose.
A bucket, plastic, used to clean floors.
Most of the materials above can be adjectives or nouns, for example a cotton shirt, or a shirt made of cotton, a leather sofa, or a sofa made of leather.
The exception is wood: a chair made of wood is a wooden chair.

tracks.title: Unit 63B - Shapes and colours
sentences: When we want to say that a shape is 'almost round' or a colour is 'a sort of green', we can also express this idea with the suffix -ish, for example she had a roundish face, he wore a greenish tie.

tracks.title: Unit 64A - Containers and contents
sentences: A bag of shopping.
A bottle of apple juice.
A jar of jam.
A bowl of sugar.
A jug of water.
A box of chocolates.
A packet of biscuits.
A packet of crisps.
A can of cola.
A carton of orange juice.
A tube of toothpaste.
A vase of flowers.
A tin of fruit.
A bar of chocolate.
A tin usually has something we eat inside it.
A can has something we drink.

tracks.title: Unit 64B - Quantities
sentences: Two sheets of paper.
A spoonful of sugar.
A bunch of keys.
Three slices of beef.
Just a drop of milk and no sugar in my coffee, please.
I've cut three slices of beef.
That should be enough for the sandwiches.
I took my boss a bunch of flowers when I visited her in hospital.
I need a sheet of paper.
Andrew bought two pairs of trousers and a pair of shoes yesterday.
I bought a couple of books.
We saw several churches on our way here.
I bought half a dozen eggs at the supermarket.
There are a dozen students in the class.
Could I have a spoonful of sugar in my tea?
We've got plenty of time.
The company is now making the toys in large quantities.
We can usually use slice when talking about pieces of bread, cakes and meat.
We can use bunch to talk about flowers, grapes, bananas and keys.

tracks.title: Unit 65A - Apologies with common replies
sentences: We can apologise in different ways in different situations.
I'm sorry – I've forgotten your book.
Never mind. That's OK.
I beg your pardon – I didn't see you there.
That's all right.
I'm sorry to disturb you.
Don't worry. Come on in. I can finish this later.
I'm sorry to keep you waiting – I won't be long.
OK. Fine.
Excuse me, I won't be a minute.
I must apologise for the noise last night.
That's all right. I understand.
I'm really sorry I'm late.
Don't worry.
Terribly/really makes you sound 'more sorry'.
I beg your pardon is a more formal apology, often used if you walk into someone.
We use this phrase when we interrupt or speak to someone who is busy working.
We use this phrase when someone is waiting for us.
I won't be long means I will be with you very soon.
We use this phrase when we have to leave a room or go somewhere.
This is a more formal apology, and it is often used in business letters.

tracks.title: Unit 65B - Excuses
sentences: We often give an explanation or excuse after an apology.
An excuse is a reason for the apology, which may or may not be true.
These are excuses students might give for being late for class.
I'm sorry I'm late for class – there was a delay on the underground.
I'm sorry I'm late for class – I was held up in traffic.
I'm sorry I'm late for class – my train was cancelled, and I had to wait half an hour for the next one.
I'm sorry I'm late for class – I overslept.

tracks.title: Unit 65C - Thanks and replies
sentences: These are some common ways of thanking people, with typical replies.
Thanks very much.
I've brought your books.
Not at all.
Oh, cheers.
No problem.
I'll post those letters for you.
Oh, thank you. That's very kind of you.

tracks.title: Unit 66A - Requests and replies
sentences: Could you pass the salt?
No problem.
Could you possibly help me?
Yes, of course.
Naomi, I wonder if you could help me?
Sure.
I was wondering if you could lend me some money until tomorrow.
No, I'm afraid I can't.
As the requests become bigger it is normal to use longer phrases which sound more polite.

tracks.title: Unit 66B - Asking permission and replies
sentences: Could I possibly have a look at your magazine?
Yes, help yourself.
May I open the window?
Sure, go ahead.
Do you mind if I watch TV?
No, go ahead.
When we answer go ahead or help yourself, we are giving someone permission to do the thing they have asked.

tracks.title: Unit 66C - Suggestions and replies
sentences: What shall we do tonight?
Let's go to the café in the square.
We could try that new restaurant.
How about going to a club?
I don't mind. You choose.
Why don't we go and see a film?
Do you fancy listening to some music?
We can reply with different answers, from positive to negative.
Yeah, that's a great idea.
Yes, if you like.
No, I don't fancy that.
That sounds good.
Yeah, I don't mind.
Mm, I'd rather do something else.

tracks.title: Unit 67A - Asking someone for their opinion
sentences: What do you think of his new book?
What do you think about global warming?
How do you feel about working with the others?

tracks.title: Unit 67B - Introducing your own opinion
sentences: Personally, I think Helena was probably right.
Personally, I feel that we should increase the price.
In my opinion, we need to change the direction of the company.
My view is that we need to wait a bit longer.

tracks.title: Unit 67C - Giving the opinion of others
sentences: The newspaper says that his death was not an accident.
According to the paper, the government didn't know about it.

tracks.title: Unit 67D - Agreeing and disagreeing (with someone)
sentences: I totally agree with you.
I partly agree with you.
I agree with you to a certain extent.
In British English, it is common to agree with someone before giving a different opinion.
That's true, but I think.
I see what you mean.
I agree to some extent.
I think that's a good point.
I take your point.

tracks.title: Unit 67E - Giving a strong opinion
sentences: I feel very strongly about military service.
I think everyone should do it.
I think you're absolutely right.
I think you're absolutely wrong.
I don't agree at all.
I disagree completely.

tracks.title: Unit 68A - Likes and dislikes
sentences: I love rock music.
So do I.
Really? I don't.
I'm really into dance music.
So am I.
Really? I'm not.
I like a lot of pop music.
Me too.
Do you? I hate it.
I quite like salsa and samba.
Yeah, it's OK.
Oh, I'm not very keen.
I don't mind jazz.
Oh, I can't stand it.
I'm not very keen on folk music.
Neither am I.
Really? I love it.
I can't stand classical music.
Neither can I.
Really? I quite like it.
I hate opera.
Many of these verbs can be followed by a noun or an -ing form, for example I love driving, I like singing, I don't mind work, I dislike driving, I can't stand getting up early, I hate cold weather.

tracks.title: Unit 68B - Attitudes and interests
sentences: My attitude to a lot of things has changed over the years.
I used to like chips.
I used to play computer games, but now they don't interest me.
I'm very interested in modern architecture, but I used to think it was awful.
I used to go swimming a lot, but now I don't go at all.
Mark and I used to have the same interests, but now it seems we have nothing in common.
It takes time to get used to living in a different country.
I didn't like my new glasses at first, but now I've got used to them.
I found the winters very cold at first, but you get used to it.

tracks.title: Unit 68C - Which do you, or would you, prefer?
sentences: Which do you prefer in general, tea or coffee?
Well, I prefer coffee to tea in the morning, but in the afternoon I usually drink tea.
I think I'd prefer to go to the cinema.
Tonight, we can go to the cinema or the theatre.
Which would you prefer?

tracks.title: Unit 69A - Greetings
sentences: When we are introduced to a stranger in a formal situation, we usually shake hands and say hello, or perhaps hello, nice to meet you.
In an informal situation, we usually just say hello or hi.
When we greet friends, there are no real rules in Britain.
Men may kiss female friends on one cheek, on both cheeks, or not at all.
Women may kiss male friends and female friends once, twice, or not at all.
Hello. How are you?
Fine, thanks. How about you?
Hi. How's it going?
Not so bad, thanks. And you?

tracks.title: Unit 69B - Farewells
sentences: To someone we have just met for the first time we can say Goodbye. Nice to meet you.
With friends we can say bye, cheers, take care, or see you soon.
Have a nice weekend.
Yeah, same to you.

tracks.title: Unit 69C - Expressions for special situations
sentences: Excuse me, could I just get past?
Yes, of course.
Sue found my wallet and my credit cards.
Oh, thank goodness for that.
Bless you.
It's your exam today, isn't it?
Good luck.
Thanks, I'll need it.
I hear you passed your exam. Congratulations.
Thanks.
Cheers.
We use Thank goodness when we are happy that something bad did not happen.
A synonym is Thank God, but there are some people who may not like the use of this expression.
We can use Goodness on its own to express surprise, for example Goodness, is it ten o'clock already?

tracks.title: Unit 70A - With adjectives
sentences: Prefixes, for example un-, dis-, im-, can be added to some adjectives to give the opposite meaning.
Happy, unhappy.
Honest, dishonest.
Possible, impossible.
Correct, incorrect.
Regular, irregular.
Legal, illegal.
Dan used all the milk but said he didn't.
He's very dishonest.
I got eight answers right, but two were incorrect.
It's illegal in the UK to ride a motorbike without a helmet.

tracks.title: Unit 70B - un-
sentences: Of the prefixes above, un- is the most common, and appears in a number of adjectives.
This chair is incredibly uncomfortable.
It was unnecessary for them to wait for us.
Xerxes – that's a very unusual name.
I won the game.
It was completely unexpected.
Marsha's hat is unbelievable.
We tried to open the door, but we were unable to get in.
I need to do more exercise.
I'm very unfit.
Declan played well, and I thought he was unlucky to lose.
They're unlikely to get here before midday.
The test was unfair because some of the students had more time to do it than others.
Adding a negative prefix does not usually change the pronunciation.
The stress stays the same.
She was unlucky.
It's unbelievable.

tracks.title: Unit 70C - With verbs
sentences: With some verbs, these prefixes can have particular meanings.
I disagree with the others.
The plane appeared in the sky, then it disappeared behind a cloud.
I couldn't unlock the door this morning.
We had to get undressed in the cold.
I unpacked the bags.
The bank overcharged me.
I misunderstood what he said.
I'm afraid my English isn't very good.
I misheard her.
I thought she said Rita, not Brita.
The teacher has asked me to rewrite my essay.

tracks.title: Unit 71A - Verb + noun suffix
sentences: Suffixes are used at the ends of words.
They often tell you if a word is a noun, a verb, an adjective or an adverb.
Two suffixes which form nouns from verbs are -ion and -ment.
Invent, invention.
Discuss, discussion.
Translate, translation.
Relax, relaxation.
Improve, improvement.
Govern, government.
Manage, management.
Develop, development.
His latest invention is a new board game.
The management has to change.
Relaxation will help you to sleep better.
I need money for more development.
We had a discussion about politics.
We need an improvement in the weather.

tracks.title: Unit 71B - Adjective + noun suffix
sentences: The suffixes -ness and -ity often form nouns from adjectives.
Weak, weakness.
Happy, happiness.
Ill, illness.
Fit, fitness.
Stupid, stupidity.
Popular, popularity.
Similar, similarity.
Able, ability.
What is his main weakness as a manager?
Fortunately it wasn't a serious illness.
Snowboarding is growing in popularity.
There is a similarity between them.
I'm frustrated by my inability to use computers.
Her happiness is all that matters to me.

tracks.title: Unit 71C - -er, -or and -ist
sentences: These suffixes can be added to nouns or verbs.
They often describe people and jobs.
Ballet dancer.
Company director.
Artist, for example Picasso.
Pop singer.
Translator.
Economist.
Professional footballer.
Film actor.
Scientist.
Adding a suffix to a verb, adjective or noun may change the position of the main stress, for example relax – relaxation, stupid – stupidity, economics – economist.

tracks.title: Unit 72A - -al
sentences: Common suffixes that form adjectives include: -able (comfortable), -al (musical), -y (cloudy), -ous (famous) and -ive (attractive).
Adjectives ending -al are usually formed from nouns, and often mean 'relating to the noun', for example a musical instrument, a political issue, an electrical fault, a personal opinion.
Shichi-Go-San (meaning 7, 5, 3) is an annual ceremony in Japan for three-and seven-year-old girls, and three-and five-year-old boys.
It is on November 15, but it is not a national holiday, so people often have it on the nearest weekend.
Seven, five and three are seen as important years in the growth of a child, and the numbers also have cultural significance because odd numbers (1, 3, 5, 7, 9, etc.) are lucky in Japan.
This is one of the special days when the older boys and girls are allowed to wear traditional clothes: a kimono for girls and hakama trousers for boys.
Now it is quite normal for children to wear western-style clothes at this ceremony.
The children visit a shrine and are then given special sweets called Chitose-Ame by their parents.

tracks.title: Unit 72B - -able
sentences: This suffix forms adjectives from nouns and verbs, and the prefix un- often forms the opposite.
An enjoyable party.
A comfortable chair.
A suitable word or phrase.
A reliable service.
Reliable information.
Fashionable clothes.
A reasonable decision or price.
A sociable person.
Sometimes the opposite form is the more common adjective.
An unforgettable experience.
An unbelievable story.

tracks.title: Unit 72C - -ful and -less
sentences: The suffix -ful often means 'full of' or 'having the quality of the noun', for example a colourful room has a lot of colour in it.
A helpful person gives a lot of help.
A peaceful place is calm and quiet.
The opposite meaning is sometimes formed by adding the suffix -less to the noun.
A useful machine.
A painful injection.
A careful driver.

tracks.title: Unit 73A - Formation
sentences: We form compound nouns by putting two words together (or three) to create a new idea.
Compound nouns are very common in English.
Ice hockey is a game of hockey played on ice.
Public transport is transport that can be used by the public.
A campsite is a site for camping.
A babysitter is someone who looks after young children when their parents go out.
Income tax is money you pay to the government out of your income.
Your mother tongue is the first language you learn as a child.
A travel agent is someone whose job is to make travel arrangements for you.
A film-maker is someone who makes films for the cinema or television.
A full stop is a punctuation mark at the end of a sentence.
A haircut is the act of cutting someone's hair, for example that boy needs a haircut.
DJ, disc jockey.
Crossroads.
Hairdresser.
Sunglasses.
Hairdryer.
Wheelchair.
Cashpoint.
Traffic lights.
Toothpaste.
Chest of drawers.
Toothbrush.
Earrings.

tracks.title: Unit 73B - One word or two?
sentences: Some compound nouns are written as one word, for example wheelchair.
A few are written with a hyphen, for example T-shirt.
Many are written as two words, for example credit card, bus stop.
Use a dictionary to check if necessary.

tracks.title: Unit 73C - Pronunciation
sentences: The main stress is usually on the first part, for example haircut or income tax, but sometimes it is on the second part, for example public transport, full stop.
Use a dictionary to check if necessary.

tracks.title: Unit 73D - Forming new compounds
sentences: One part of a compound often forms the basis for a number of compound nouns.
Airport, for example Heathrow.
Airline, for example Lufthansa, KLM.
Aircraft, for example Airbus A380.
Bus driver.
ID card, identity card.
Bus stop.
Credit card.
Bus station.
Birthday card.

tracks.title: Unit 74A - What do we mean by word partners?
sentences: If you want to use a word naturally, you often need to know other words that are commonly used with it.
These are called word partners or collocations, and they can be different from language to language.
I spent time in Paris.
I missed the bus.
We have children.
I made a mistake.
It was a serious injury.
It's quite likely that we'll stay here.
It depends on my parents.
More word partners can be found in the Cambridge Learner's Dictionary for intermediate learners and the Cambridge Advanced Learner's Dictionary.

tracks.title: Unit 74B - Verbs and nouns
sentences: There are many verbs and nouns that are often used together.
Pavel told me a joke but I can't remember it.
I need to lose weight.
The doctor told my father that he should go on a diet.
It's very difficult to predict the future.
We won the game 2–1, and my brother scored the first goal.
My neighbour's alarm went off in the middle of the night.
They fell in love and got married.
Now Emily is expecting a baby.

tracks.title: Unit 74C - Adjectives and nouns
sentences: Many adjectives and nouns are often used together.
These are all examples where the meaning is 'big' or 'a lot', but where we usually use a particular adjective.
We've got a large number of students this year.
I will need a large amount of money.
There's a wide choice of food available.
Sergio has a wide vocabulary in English.
It's only a toy gun but it makes a very loud noise.
Her family is of great importance to her.
We went to a party last night and it was great fun.
There was heavy traffic on the motorway going out of town.

tracks.title: Unit 74D - Adverbs and adjectives
sentences: The adverbs in the first three sentences mean very, the adverbs in the last sentence mean fully or completely.
In each case, the adverbs and adjectives are common word partners.
I'm terribly sorry I'm late.
She's well aware of the problem.
It's vitally important that we get the right person for the job.
Ava is fast asleep, but the other two are still wide awake.

tracks.title: Unit 75A - What are fixed phrases?
sentences: There are many groups of words which you need to learn as complete phrases, because they often have a meaning which is different from the words on their own.
Many of these are called idioms.
They arrived out of the blue.
Does the name Merchant ring a bell?
You'll have to make up your mind soon.
Some fixed phrases are easier to understand, but the same idea may be expressed in a different way in your language.
I think we'll have to get rid of some of this furniture.
I'm afraid I can't talk to you now; I'm just about to leave.
I don't know what Martin said, but Natalie burst into tears.
She was very upset.
Everyone was there apart from Yasmine.

tracks.title: Unit 75B - Time phrases
sentences: He's leaving in four days' time.
I got here the week before last.
The course is fine so far.
I rang our local doctor and fortunately she was able to come at once.
He's so busy at work; he comes home late night after night.

tracks.title: Unit 75C - Pairs of words
sentences: A number of fixed phrases consist of two words, usually joined by and, and sometimes or.
I go to the theatre now and again.
Andy had one or two suggestions.
I've got a house in the country, and I like to go there whenever possible for a bit of peace and quiet.
I've been up and down the street, but I can't find their house.
I've been to Paris once or twice.
I'll finish this report sooner or later.

tracks.title: Unit 75D - Introducing advice, opinions, etc.
sentences: Many fixed phrases are used to introduce advice, an opinion, an example, etc.
If I were you, I'd accept the job they offered you.
In general the summers are quite warm in this part of the country.
There were lots of questions.
For instance, how much will it cost?
I'm sure you'll have a great holiday.
By the way, what time does the train leave?
To be honest, I didn't like her boyfriend very much.

tracks.title: Unit 76A - Common responses
sentences: A large number of fixed phrases are used as common responses in everyday conversation.
What time did he get here?
I've no idea.
Are you going tonight?
That depends.
Shall we go out tonight?
Yeah, why not?
Did you like the film?
Not really.
I'm going to Sweden next week.
Really.
What for?
Can I borrow your car?
No way.
They're coming tonight, aren't they?
I suppose so.
Jason has grown a beard.
I know.
I couldn't believe my eyes!
We have to work tonight.
You must be joking.
Eli got top marks.
I don't believe it!
We can use you must be joking when someone has just said something that is a surprise.
It may be an unpleasant surprise, or a nice surprise.
My father said he would pay for all of us to go on holiday.
You're kidding.
Fantastic!

tracks.title: Unit 76B - In conversation
sentences: Notice how fixed phrases can form an important part of an everyday conversation.
I had to have a word with Vince and Milan today.
Oh yeah?
Are they causing trouble again?
Well, you know, they just keep talking to each other, and they don't pay attention - that sort of thing.
Why don't you make them sit in different places?
I tried that, but they still talked to each other.
Well, in that case, you'll have to move one of them.
Maybe, but I'd feel bad about that.
They're quite nice boys, you know, they just can't help it.
Sure, but you can't let that kind of thing continue.
No, you're right.
But I've given them a final warning today, so let's wait and see what happens.
OK.
But what if they don't improve?
I think it'll be OK.
Well, if you change your mind, come and see me again, and I'll arrange for one of them to be moved to another class.
We use sort/kind in several common phrases in informal speech.
I grow onions and carrots - that sort/kind of thing.
Her new top is sort of blue.
I thought he was kind of strange.

tracks.title: Unit 77A - Verb + preposition
sentences: Pay special attention to prepositions that are different in your language.
Paul doesn't like the manager's decision and I agree with him.
Don't worry about your exam.
His teachers were satisfied with his progress.
Many people spend a lot of money on clothes.
I'm thinking of going to China.
This land belongs to the company.
I translated the letter into French.
She complained to the manager about the food.
I can't concentrate on my work when the radio is playing.
We can rely on this photocopier.
I may go but it depends on the weather.
Martha depends on her son for money.
We can depend on the others for support.
Remember: it depends on something or someone.

tracks.title: Unit 77B - Prepositions that change the meaning
sentences: She shouted to me.
He shouted at me.
He threw the ball to me.
He threw the ball at me.

tracks.title: Unit 77C - Adjective + preposition
sentences: I've never been very good at maths.
She's afraid of flying.
The neighbours are fond of the children.
She's similar to her sister, but very different from her brother.
He's very interested in photography.
I think she's aware of the problem.
I'm tired of people telling me what to do.
Katya is mad about Brad Pitt.
We're short of coffee at the moment.
There's something wrong with this TV.

tracks.title: Unit 78A - By, on or in + noun
sentences: I took his pen by mistake.
These shoes are made by hand.
I met them by chance.
He broke the plate by accident.
He broke the plate on purpose.
My boss is on the phone at the moment.
The workers are on strike.
The books were on display in the window.
Part of the building was on fire.
Most of the passengers are now on board.
Why are they always in a hurry?
I explained everything in detail.
She won't make that mistake again in future.
The poor little girl was in tears.

tracks.title: Unit 78B - Phrases easily confused
sentences: Sometimes two prepositions can be used with the same noun, but the meaning is different.
Lessons begin at 8.30 and the students are usually here on time.
If we hurry, we'll be there in time.
We were tired of waiting, so in the end we went home.
At the end of the book they get married.
To be successful in business it's important to get on well with people.
They're both in Germany on business.
I'm afraid this book's a bit out-of-date.
I try to keep up-to-date with all the changes.
I'll see you in a moment.
I can't speak to you at the moment.

tracks.title: Unit 79A - Formation
sentences: A phrasal verb is a verb + adverb or preposition, and sometimes a verb + adverb and preposition.
He fell over and hurt his knee.
I'll try to find out the quickest way to get there.
He didn't like his coat, so he gave it away.
If you don't understand the meaning, look it up.
Who's going to sort out the problem?
He doesn't get on with his parents.

tracks.title: Unit 79B - Meaning
sentences: The adverb or preposition does not always change the meaning of the verb, and is not always used.
I didn't wake up until 7 o'clock.
She's saving up for a new computer.
Hurry up or we'll be late.
I went to lie down on the bed.

tracks.title: Unit 79C - Multiple meanings
sentences: Sometimes an adverb adds a particular meaning to the verb.
For example, back can mean return.
I bought this jacket yesterday, but I'm going to take it back to the shop; it's too small.
You can look at the books, but remember to put them back on the shelf.
They liked Greece so much they want to go back next year.
Often, the adverb or preposition changes the meaning of the verb: give up doesn't mean the same as give, and carry on doesn't mean the same as carry.
My wife has decided to give up smoking.
We'll take a short break and then carry on with the meeting.
The shops are going to put up the price of bread.
When the hotel gets busy, we have to take on more staff.
Many phrasal verbs have more than one meaning.
I was busy, so I told him to go away.
We try to go away in August.
I picked up most of the rubbish.
I'm going to pick Jane up at the station.
Could you put the light on?
I put on my best suit.

tracks.title: Unit 80A - Phrasal verbs with no object
sentences: Some phrasal verbs don't have an object.
We cannot put other words between the parts of the verb.
When does your train get in?
Hold on, I just need to get my coat.
The car broke down on my way to work.
I'll call for them at 8 o'clock.
We set off about 7.30.

tracks.title: Unit 80B - Phrasal verbs with an object
sentences: Many phrasal verbs need an object.
We can usually put the object in different positions.
Put on your shoes.
Turn on the TV.
Take off your coat.
Put your shoes on.
Turn the TV on.
Take your coat off.
These are separable phrasal verbs, but if the object is a long phrase, it usually goes at the end.
I turned off the lights in the living room.
If the object is a pronoun, it must go in the middle.
Turn it off.
There are some phrasal verbs where the object must come after the phrasal verb.
I will look after the children for you.
How did the two men break into the shop?

tracks.title: Unit 80C - In dictionaries
sentences: Dictionaries usually show the grammar of a phrasal verb like this.
To spend time somewhere, usually without doing very much.
There's nowhere for teenagers to go, so they just hang around on street corners.
To give something to all the people in a group.
A girl was handing out leaflets at the station.
To begin to feel better after being unhappy or ill.
It took her months to get over the shock of Richard leaving.

tracks.title: Unit 80D - Style
sentences: Phrasal verbs are commonly used in spoken and written English.
Many phrasal verbs are quite informal, and sometimes there is a single word with the same meaning as a phrasal verb, which sounds more formal.
We use this word instead of the phrasal verb in more formal situations.
He made a list of people but left me out.
We had to make up a story.
They put the game off till Friday.
I offered her a job but she turned it down.

tracks.title: Unit 81A - Things we make, do and take
sentences: We use all three of these common verbs with particular nouns.
These word partners (collocations) are often different in other languages, so look at them carefully.
I've made a mistake.
I'm afraid I haven't done my homework.
She took a photo of me this morning.
I haven't done the housework yet.
They made a big effort to finish the work.
The children are making too much noise.
Sometimes two different verbs are possible.
I'm just going to take/have a shower.
I'm doing/taking a Spanish course next month.
Take/Have a look at these pictures.
I don't do/take much exercise.
We must make/take a decision.

tracks.title: Unit 81B - Make
sentences: Make can mean 'to cause something to happen or cause a particular state'.
I like Boris - he makes me laugh.
It made them angry when Karen refused to help with the washing-up.
Make can also mean 'to force someone to do something'.
My parents made me do my homework when I was a child.
The police made us wait outside the main gates.
If you make friends with someone, you get to know them and like them.
I made friends with a couple of guys from Canada when I was on holiday.
If you make up your mind, you decide something.
He's made up his mind to leave work at the end of next month.

tracks.title: Unit 81C - Do
sentences: We use do to describe a general action when we don't know what it is, or there's no noun.
What shall we do this afternoon?
Don't just stand there, do something.
If you do your hair / make-up, you make it look nice.
I'll just do my hair, then we can go out.
If you do your best, you try as hard as possible.
I'm not a good student, but I always do my best.

tracks.title: Unit 81D - Take
sentences: I took the key out of my pocket.
Someone has taken my pen.
Did he take the job they offered him?
Do they take credit cards there?
I took notes during the meeting.
The man took my name and address.
I take size 43 shoes.
If you take it / things easy, you relax and don't do very much.
I'll take things easy today.
If you take a break, you stop work and rest for a short period.
Let's take a ten-minute break.

tracks.title: Unit 82A - Give
sentences: We can use give when someone or something causes a certain effect.
All that noise has given me a headache.
The walk this morning gave me an appetite.
Listening to Wai Sin has given me an idea.
We often use give with different nouns to express an action.
He gave me some advice.
Valentina gave us a shock when she shouted like that.
I had to give a speech at the wedding.
I'll give her a ring.
I had lots of work to do, but fortunately Ollie gave me a hand.
She gave me a hug.
He gave the car a push.

tracks.title: Unit 82B - Keep
sentences: Keep can mean to stay or cause someone to stay in a particular state or condition.
Keep right.
She asked us to keep quiet.
This coat will keep you warm.
Going to the gym keeps me fit.
Keep on doing something means to repeat doing something, and often it is something you don't want to do or happen.
I keep losing my glasses.
I keep getting backache.
She keeps on interrupting me.
If you keep in touch, you continue to communicate with someone, especially by phone or email.
I met Mara on holiday and we've kept in touch ever since.
If you keep a secret, you don't tell other people something that you know.
Tom told me not to tell anyone about his new job, but I'm not very good at keeping a secret.
If you keep something up, you continue doing something at a high level.
You're working hard and your English is improving.
That's good - keep it up.

tracks.title: Unit 82C - Miss
sentences: If you miss a person, you feel sad because that person is not there.
When I went to work in Hungary, I really missed my girlfriend.
If you miss what someone says, you don't hear it.
I'm sorry, I missed that.
What did you say?
If you miss a chance / opportunity, you don't use an opportunity to do something.
Lily's been chosen for the team, but now she's injured, she might miss her chance to play.
If you miss something, you manage to avoid it or not experience it.
I left home early in order to miss the rush hour.

tracks.title: Unit 83A - Uses
sentences: Get is an informal word and is very common in spoken English.
It can have many different meanings.
I got a ticket from my brother.
I need to get some help.
I got a new phone for my birthday.
Did you get my email?
Where did you get that watch?
I went to get a magazine.
What time did you get here?
I'll phone when I get home.
It gets dark very early in winter.
My hands are getting cold.
Could you go and get my glasses from the kitchen for me?
Is that the door?
Yes, I'll get it.
I need to get my hair cut.
I'll never get this essay finished; it's too difficult.
I got my watch repaired today.

tracks.title: Unit 83B - Phrases
sentences: You can get in touch with me via email.
A bedtime story helps children get to sleep.
The salsa classes are a chance for us to get together.
I'm sorry I got the number wrong.
My cold is getting worse.
I'd like to get rid of my old CDs.
I got to know lots of Americans when I stayed in San Francisco.

tracks.title: Unit 83C - Phrasal verbs
sentences: He stopped the car and I got out.
I gave her £25, but I'll get it back tomorrow.
The door was locked so we couldn't get in.
Our train should get in around midnight.
What time did you get up this morning?

tracks.title: Unit 84A - Different meanings of go
sentences: When we leave a place in order to do an activity, especially for enjoyment, we often express it with go + -ing or go (out) for a + noun.
We could go shopping / sightseeing / swimming / clubbing tomorrow.
Why don't we go out for a walk / drive / drink / meal / picnic at lunchtime?
Go can also describe a changing state (usually to a bad one) with certain adjectives.
My dad's going grey and my uncle is going bald.
My grandmother is going deaf.
Our 12-year-old dog is going blind.
He'll go mad if you wear his jacket.
We use go when we want to ask/say if a road or form of transport takes you somewhere.
Does this bus go to the National Gallery?
I think this road goes through the village.
Go can also mean 'disappear'.
When I looked in the drawer, my watch had gone.
Go and get means fetch.
You stay here, and I'll go and get the bags from the car.

tracks.title: Unit 84B - Phrasal verbs and expressions
sentences: What's going on in here?
I don't know.
I touched this switch and the lights went out.
Shall we wait for George or go on to the theatre?
Er, I'm not sure of the way; let's wait for George.
How's the business?
Well, it was going well up until the summer, but since then a few things have gone wrong, and we've lost a few customers.
Are you going away this year?
Yes.
We had a lovely time in Italy last year, so we've decided to go back in June.
I think I'll have the chicken.
How about you?
I'm going for the roast beef.
How's it going?
Not bad.
And you?

tracks.title: Unit 85A - The five basic senses
sentences: To express it another way, the ability to see, hear, taste, smell and feel.

tracks.title: Unit 85B - Sense verbs with adjectives
sentences: You look tired this evening.
That man sounded foreign.
This cake tastes a bit strange.
Fresh bread smells wonderful.
This shirt feels damp.
The verbs above can all be used as nouns.
I like the look of this hotel.
I love the sound of his voice.

tracks.title: Unit 85C - Sense verbs with like or as if/though
sentences: We can describe things using sense verbs with like + noun or as if / though + clause, but not like + adjective.
When we use like + noun, we are often describing how similar two things are.
Have you ever had a nectarine?
They look like peaches.
Did you hear that noise?
It sounded like an alarm.
That shampoo smells like coconut.
Don't you think this material feels like silk?
That boy looks as if he's trying to get over the wall.
I spoke to Isobel.
It sounded as though they had a good time on holiday.
We also use seem and appear to describe a sense or feeling about someone or something, after we have seen them, talked to them, etc.
Before like + noun, we usually use seem.
I saw Will and he seemed/appeared quite happy.
The shop appears/seems to be very busy.
Amelia said she wanted to travel a bit, which seems like a good idea.

tracks.title: Unit 85D - Verbs easily confused
sentences: If you look at the map, you can see the church on the left.
They've been watching that man for weeks.
He ran into me because he wasn't looking.
I watched/saw a film on TV.
I saw a film at the cinema.
I heard what she said but I wasn't listening.
Don't touch the oven.
It's hot!
Just feel my feet.
Aren't they cold?

tracks.title: Unit 86A - Common uncountable nouns
sentences: One of the problems with uncountable nouns is that many of them are countable in other languages.
You can put all that rubbish in the bin over there.
Is there any more news about the man who was injured?
She gave me some good advice about buying a car.
Do the children get pocket money?
You need a lot of equipment for camping, e.g. tent, sleeping bag, torch, things for cooking, etc.
We sold the furniture.
The scenery is really beautiful.
My knowledge of Russian is limited.
She's worked very hard and I believe she is making progress.
Can you take the dog?
We haven't got any room in our car.
Would anyone like some more toast?
The children's behaviour was terrible: they were climbing all over the furniture and making a lot of noise.

tracks.title: Unit 86B - Uncountable nouns in dictionaries
sentences: Dictionaries show countable nouns with a (C) and uncountable nouns with a (U).
Some nouns can be countable with one meaning, and uncountable with another.
She's got a lot of experience of working with children.
I had so many fantastic experiences on my trip to Thailand and Japan.
Lotto is a game of chance.
He's had several chances to go abroad, but he's just not interested.

tracks.title: Unit 86C - Making uncountable nouns countable
sentences: You can make some uncountable nouns singular.
Sometimes we do this with a word like piece (for advice, equipment, toast, furniture, news), but in spoken English we often use a bit with most uncountable nouns.

tracks.title: Unit 87A - Verb + -ing form
sentences: A number of verbs are commonly followed by an -ing form.
Do you enjoy studying?
Yes, I do.
Do you mind getting up early?
No, I don't mind at all.
Do you like or dislike having your photograph taken?
I hate having my picture taken.
Do you usually avoid speaking to strangers at parties?
Yes, I prefer to talk to people that I already know.
Can you imagine being without a car?
I need my car.
Have you ever considered living in another country?
Yes, many times.
I'd like to live abroad.
Would you recommend having a holiday in the capital city of your country?
If so, would you suggest going at a particular time of the year?
Yes, I would, and I would suggest going in spring or autumn.

tracks.title: Unit 87B - Verb + to infinitive
sentences: I hope to see them next week.
They agreed to help me.
I intend to leave next month.
I offered to help them.
I attempted to cook the dinner, but it was terrible.
I promised to bring her book back.
The shop assistant was very rude, so I demanded to see the manager.

tracks.title: Unit 87C - Verb + (object) + preposition + noun/-ing
sentences: Jo has just rung and asked me for advice about Greece.
They're thinking of going there.
Well, be careful.
They went to India last year on your advice and then blamed you for the terrible holiday they had.
That was their fault.
They insisted on going in the summer when it was far too hot.

tracks.title: Unit 88A - A great opportunity
sentences: I'm 24 years old, and I work in a photography studio.
It's not a very exciting job but I love photography and the pay isn't bad.
But, two months ago, I was given the chance to go to Italy and work on a film by a famous director.
My best friend thought that it was a fantastic opportunity and advised me to go.
Dad wasn't so sure.
He didn't try and persuade me not to go, but he warned me that it would be hard work, and reminded me that it was only three months, then I'd be out of a job.
I realised that my girlfriend wasn't happy about it either, but I promised her that I would phone every day, and suggested that she could come out to Italy for a holiday while I was there.
I didn't mention that I was part of a small team with three other women.
Anyway, I'm going.
Some verbs can be followed by different constructions.
I suggested going there.
She warned me not to go.
He reminded me to post the letter.

tracks.title: Unit 88B - Other verbs
sentences: Here are some more verbs which are used with the same constructions.
I said that I was busy.
I hope that you'll come and see us soon.
When I left, I noticed that the door was open.
I recommended that we all go together, so no one gets lost.
I expect that he'll ring us later.
I told them that they could leave early.
He tried to convince me that I needed some new clothes.
Tracey asked me to look after her cat.
They told us to wait outside.
They want us to stay at school.
I expected them to be here by now.
I had to remind him to buy the food.
She helped me to write the report.
She helped me write the report.
My parents encouraged me to read.

tracks.title: Unit 89A - Extreme adjectives
sentences: There are many 'extreme' adjectives we use to say that something is very good, or very small, or very surprising, etc.
We were lucky – the weather was marvellous.
Don't go and see that film – it's awful.
I was delighted she passed her exam.
It's a nice modern flat, but it's absolutely tiny.
I wasn't very hungry, but they gave us a huge meal.
You should watch that programme; you'll find it absolutely fascinating.
Everyone was really exhausted by the end of the day.
Bungee jumping is the most terrifying thing I've ever done.
Computers are an essential part of modern life.
The food was delicious.
The food was absolutely marvellous.
We can use absolutely or really before extreme adjectives, e.g. absolutely awful, really terrific, but we can't use very.
We use very or really with gradable adjectives which do not have an extreme meaning, e.g. very big, very good, very nice, very tired, really good, really tired, etc.

tracks.title: Unit 89B - Adjectives ending in -ing and -ed
sentences: A large group of adjectives can have an -ing or -ed ending.
The -ing ending describes a person, thing or situation; the -ed ending describes the effect on someone of this person, thing or situation.
I don't know if other people were bored, but I thought it was a very boring lesson.
The weather is so depressing at the moment; it's making everyone feel depressed.
These adjectives can all end in -ing or -ed, depending on the meaning.
It was really tiring going up that hill.
I was amazed she could climb that wall.
My exam results were very disappointing.
She was annoyed that I forgot to tell her.
I kept calling her Emma, so I was embarrassed when Ben told me her name was Angela.
The map he gave us was very confusing.
We were shocked by the violence in the film.

tracks.title: Unit 90A - At, on and in
sentences: At a point or place, e.g. I met her at the bus stop.
He's at work at the moment.
On a surface, e.g. The book's on the desk.
They sat on the floor.
I put the picture on the wall.
In an area, space, or inside something, e.g. He's in the kitchen.
She lives in Warsaw/Poland.
The knife's in the top drawer.

tracks.title: Unit 90B - Where exactly?
sentences: I know they live in Danvers Street, and I think they're at number twenty-three.
I'm sure there's a chemist on the left before the bank.
Their house is beyond the farm, by the old church.
I saw your bike in the back garden against the wall.
They've just bought a house right beside/by the river.
I found your phone on the sofa beneath/underneath a pile of cushions.
Their office is above the shop.
You can just see the top of the building among the trees.

tracks.title: Unit 90C - Movement
sentences: The mouse ran out of the back door, towards the gate, then disappeared down a hole.
We came over the bridge, then through the tunnel and round the lake.
The woman came after us, but we managed to climb into the back of my dad's van.
Fortunately she went past the van and didn't see us.

tracks.title: Unit 91A - Adverbs of frequency: how often
sentences: She hardly ever plays tennis now.
I am often late.
I occasionally go to the theatre.
He rarely works at weekends.
We see them quite frequently.
I have never been to America.
Remember that frequency adverbs usually go before the main verb, with the exception of the verb be.
Notice the position of the adverb when the present perfect is used.

tracks.title: Unit 91B - Adverbs of degree: how much
sentences: I was a bit tired.
The flat was a little bit small.
She was slightly nervous.
A bit, a little and slightly have the same meaning and are mostly used before adjectives that express negative ideas, e.g. We were a bit bored.
I was slightly upset.
A bit and a little cannot be used with adjectives before a noun.
It was a slightly small flat.
The next four adverbs all mean 'more than a bit but less than very'.
The hotel was quite busy.
We had quite a nice room.
The food was fairly boring.
It was a fairly wet day.
I was rather annoyed I missed the film.
It was a rather good party.
It was rather a good party.
The weather was pretty good.
We had a pretty difficult journey.
The restaurant was completely/totally empty.
I totally/completely agree with you.
We had an extremely interesting trip.
It's an incredibly good book.

tracks.title: Unit 91C - Adverbs of manner
sentences: These adverbs describe the way in which someone does something, or the way that something happens.
Nora had secretly put all of the letters into her bag.
I went in and shut the door quietly.
The curtains were closed and the room was dark, but I suddenly realised I wasn't alone.
Penelope was in pain, and I could see she needed help urgently.
I spoke to Charles briefly this morning.
I asked him very politely if he could work an extra hour this evening, but he reacted quite angrily and walked off.

tracks.title: Unit 92A - When / As soon as
sentences: The meaning of these two time expressions is almost the same, but as soon as suggests something more immediate or important.
I'll phone my uncle when / as soon as I get home.
As soon as / When you've finished this exercise, you can go home.

tracks.title: Unit 92B - Two things happening at the same time
sentences: Violet got ready while I cooked the dinner.
The accident happened while I was on my way to work.
I saw him just as I came out of the office.

tracks.title: Unit 92C - One thing after another
sentences: We met the others in the café, and then / after that / afterwards we went to the match.
I talked to Joe, and afterwards I came home.
I was in Caracas for three months, and then / after that I went to Colombia.
After my visit to New York, I decided to have a rest.
We had something to eat before going out.

tracks.title: Unit 92D - A sequence of actions
sentences: We had a really nice holiday.
First of all / First we spent a few days in St Moritz.
After that / Then we drove down the coast and stayed in Portofino for a week.
Finally, we went back to Switzerland to stay with my uncle.

tracks.title: Unit 92E - At first … eventually
sentences: To begin with, the two girls got on very well when they shared the flat.
But after a while, they started arguing about various things, and eventually Lauren walked out and found a new place.
At first I enjoyed the classes, but after a while it got a bit boring, and in the end I left.

tracks.title: Unit 92F - A list of reasons
sentences: We can use firstly / for one thing / for a start to introduce a first reason for something, and then secondly/besides/anyway to add a further reason.
What's wrong with her new dress?
Firstly, it's a horrible colour, and secondly, I don't think it suits her.
Why can't we go out tonight?
Well, for one thing / for a start, I've got a lot of work – and besides/anyway, I can't afford it.

tracks.title: Unit 93A - As well (as), what's more, in addition (to), besides
sentences: We often link ideas using and, e.g. The food's nice and very good value.
There are other words and phrases we also use to add more information.
Sometimes we still include and or also.
The restaurant has excellent food; it's also very good value.
You always get a good view, and the seats are comfortable as well.
As well as getting cheaper tickets, I also got the opportunity to buy them in advance.
The clothes are nice, and what's more, the shop is open every day of the week.
The scheme gives young people experience.
In addition, companies can afford to employ them.
In addition to the new food department, they're also planning to open a café.
Besides being a mum with four children, she's also a successful designer.

tracks.title: Unit 93B - Although, despite, in spite of
sentences: We use these link words when there are two ideas in a sentence, and the second is surprising or not expected.
They can be used at the beginning or in the middle of the sentence.
Although / Even though the sun was shining, it wasn't very warm.
We found the place quite easily, although / even though we didn't know where it was.
They went for a walk despite the fact that it was raining.
Despite having no money, he still seemed very happy.
They got there on time in spite of the delay.
In spite of all the problems, we still enjoyed the trip.
We can use still to emphasise that we didn't expect something to happen or be true, e.g. He didn't do any work but he still passed the exam.
The work is very hard, but he still enjoys it.

tracks.title: Unit 93C - However, yet, though
sentences: We can use however and yet when the second part of an idea is surprising after the first part.
However is often used to link ideas in two separate sentences.
Though can be used in a similar way in spoken English, but usually comes at the end of the sentence.
Notice the use of commas here, and the different positions of however.
I don't agree with a lot of his methods.
However, he is a very good teacher.
We didn't particularly like the house.
The garden, however, was wonderful.
It was warm and sunny when we were there.
Most of the time, however, it's quite cold.
We went in the autumn, yet it was still quite warm.
I didn't like the film much.
I'm glad I went to see it, though.
They told us the shop was next to the station.
We never found it, though.

tracks.title: Unit 93D - While and whereas
sentences: We can use while and whereas to compare two different facts or situations.
Alex is very quick to understand, whereas/while the others are quite slow.
I get £20 an hour, while Josh only gets £12.
The speed limit on this road is 80kph, whereas it's 130kph on the motorway.
I was very keen on the film, whereas Christoph didn't like it at all.

tracks.title: Unit 94A - Reason
sentences: I went home early because I was feeling tired.
As/Since I was feeling tired, I went home early.
I was feeling tired, so I went home early.
The reason I went home early was that I was feeling tired.
We can also use because of with a different construction.
They go there because the weather is wonderful.
They go there because of the wonderful weather.
Due to means the same as because of, and is often used to explain the reason for a problem.
The plane was late due to bad weather.

tracks.title: Unit 94B - Purpose
sentences: A purpose is an intention or reason for doing something.
The purpose of the meeting is to plan next year's timetable.
We often introduce a purpose using so that or in order to.
I bought this book so that I could improve my English.
They went home early in order to watch the match on television.
We moved house so that we could send our children to this school.
She went into town in order to do some shopping.

tracks.title: Unit 94C - Result
sentences: These link words/phrases are used when one thing happens because another thing has happened.
Therefore and as a result are more formal than so, and less common in spoken English.
I left my ticket at home, so I had to buy another one.
They've got more money, and therefore they can afford to buy the best football players.
I forgot to send the email.
As a result, no one knew about the meeting.

tracks.title: Unit 94D - Conditions
sentences: We sometimes use whether when we are not sure about something.
I didn't buy it because I wasn't sure whether you'd like it.
I spoke to the others, but I don't know whether they're coming.
We'll be late unless we leave now.
Unless the weather improves, we won't be able to go.
I have to go now, otherwise I'll miss the last bus.
You'll have to turn up the music, otherwise they won't be able to hear it.
You can borrow it as long as you bring it back by Thursday.
You can wear what you like as long as you look quite smart.
I'm taking my umbrella in case it rains.
Take some money in case you need to get a taxi.

tracks.title: Unit 95A - Formal English
sentences: Formal English is more common in writing, but you will also hear examples in more formal spoken English, e.g. announcements, speeches, television news, or discussions.
Only food purchased here may be eaten on the premises.
The man is being questioned regarding the robbery last night.
I regret to inform you that we are unable to …
The play will commence in two minutes.
If you require further assistance, please contact the above address.
The next train to depart from platform 7 will be the 7:22 to Reading.
Will passengers for Miami please proceed to gate 36.
We are not in a position to grant you a visa to this country.

tracks.title: Unit 95B - Informal English
sentences: Informal language is more common in spoken English, and also in most emails or letters to friends.
The words and phrases in bold in these dialogues are all informal.
Who's Callum?
A mate of mine.
Yeah, I see him quite a bit; he's a nice bloke.
Toby, I'm afraid I can't make it this evening.
Oh, that's a shame.
Yeah, I'm sorry, but the thing is, Ella's not well, so I'll have to look after the kids.
OK, don't worry.
There will still be twelve or so at the meeting, and I'll ring you later and let you know what happens.
Cheers, that would be great.
What are you up to this evening?
Nothing much, why?
Well, would you like to see the new Coen Brothers film?
I've heard it's great.
Really, my brother saw it and said it was a load of rubbish.
I bet you're hungry.
Yes, I'm dying for something to eat.
Well, I think you'll find some stuff in the fridge.
We often use the uncountable noun stuff, especially in spoken English, to refer to an uncountable noun or a group of things.
We do this when others know what we are talking about, or if we don't need to be exact.
Put this stuff in the cupboard.
We carried our camping stuff.
What's this stuff in the fridge in the blue bottle?

tracks.title: Unit 96A - Types of form
sentences: A registration form, where you enter your name on an official list, e.g. at a school or college.
An entry form, if you want to enter for an exam, e.g. Cambridge English: First.
A landing card, for people from some countries when they enter the UK.
A visa application form, when you make an official request to enter or leave some countries.

tracks.title: Unit 96B - Language of forms
sentences: When you fill in a form, you will see that they often have more formal expressions.
In spoken English, ideas may be expressed differently.
Date of birth, when were you born?
Place of birth, where were you born?
Country of origin, where do you come from?
Marital status, are you single or married?
Date of arrival, when did you arrive?
Date of departure, when are you leaving, or when did you leave?
Signed, write your signature.

tracks.title: Unit 96C - Curriculum vitae
sentences: If you apply for a job, you need to send a letter and a CV, which should give:
Personal details, information about you such as your name, address, email address, etc.
Details about your education and qualifications, e.g. university degree, teaching certificate, etc.
Your work experience, the jobs you have done.
Your interests, what you enjoy doing.
Skills, abilities you have learned and practised, e.g. ability to speak a foreign language.
Career aims, what you want to do in your future working life.
Names of people who will give you a reference, a letter written by someone who knows you which says if you are suitable for a particular job.

If you apply to university, they require a personal statement in which you must explain why you want to go to this university; why you want to follow this particular course; details of your educational background; your skills; your interests.

tracks.title: Unit 96D - Tips for writing a CV or personal statement
sentences: A tip is a useful piece of advice.
Here are some tips for writing a CV or personal statement.
A CV should be no longer than two pages; a personal statement no more than 45 lines of text.
Type your CV or personal statement.
Handwriting is not suitable.
Keep it simple.
Don't make it complicated.
Check there are no mistakes.
Make sure the information you give is relevant.
For example, if you are going to be working on your own, don't say that you are good at working in a team.

tracks.title: Unit 97A - The basis of a discursive essay
sentences: In a discursive essay you have to express your own ideas and point of view.
It is also important to show that you understand both sides of an argument.
This means you need to understand and use different link words and phrases such as in addition, however, etc.

tracks.title: Unit 97B - Expressing a point of view
sentences: Some people believe that no one should be sent to prison under the age of 18.
There is an argument that everyone should have a university education.

You can express a personal point of view with phrases such as I believe or think that, or it seems to me that, but you can also use less personal and direct ways of expressing a point of view, as in the phrases on the left.
Many people think it is better not to use In my opinion in written essays.

tracks.title: Unit 97C - Giving both sides of an argument
sentences: One of the advantages of being an only child is that you have more attention from your parents.
However, it can be lonely without the company of brothers and sisters.
On the one hand, computers can do so many things faster than human beings.
On the other hand, some people are becoming dependent on them, which is not a good thing.

tracks.title: Unit 97D - Comparing and contrasting
sentences: We often make comparisons between groups of people, or between the past and the present.
Compared with / to my grandparents, I have had much more opportunity to travel abroad.
In the past people didn't have computers, but nowadays there is one in almost every home.
Most parts of the developed world have become richer in the last thirty years.
In contrast, many countries in Africa have become much poorer.

tracks.title: Unit 97E - Making generalisations
sentences: Sometimes a simple statement is not accurate, e.g. Young people prefer to watch American films.
This is not true for all young people, so we use certain words and phrases to show that we are making a generalisation.
In general, Japanese society is more focused on groups than individuals.
Teenagers tend to have more freedom than in the past.

tracks.title: Unit 97F - Cause and effect
sentences: Poor diet and lack of exercise are the main causes of obesity.
Obesity is often the result of a bad diet and not enough exercise.
People don't eat the right food or get enough exercise, and consequently they put on weight.
Poor diet can cause obesity, and this can have a bad effect on people's health.
Don't confuse the verb affect and the noun effect.
Pollution can affect people's breathing.
Pollution can have an effect on people's breathing.

tracks.title: Unit 98A - A formal letter
sentences: I am writing in response to your advertisement about job opportunities for graduates.
I have just completed a degree in Economics at Durham University, and I would be grateful if you could send me further details of the graduate training schemes you mention.
I am available for interview at any time.
I look forward to hearing from you.

tracks.title: Unit 98B - Useful words and phrases
sentences: Thank you for your letter regarding the damage to your vehicle.
I regret to inform you that your application has not been successful.
I am pleased to inform you that your application has been successful.
I am writing to enquire about English courses at your school.
You will need to confirm the booking in writing.
We would like to thank you for offering your support.
We would like to apologise for the delay.
Please accept our apologies for the delay.
I enclose a cheque for £100.

tracks.title: Unit 99A - An email
sentences: Hi Beth.
Just a quick message to thank you for dinner last night.
Absolutely delicious, as always, and I really enjoyed meeting your friend Alice.
She's a laugh, isn't she?
I'm hoping to get tickets for the film festival next week, so I'll be in touch to see if there's anything you fancy seeing.
Give my regards to Conrad when he gets back from Poland, and once again, thanks for last night.
Love, Sophie.

tracks.title: Unit 99B - Messaging
sentences: Hi Emma.
Sorry it's been ages since I last got in touch, but I just wanted to let you know that I'm coming to Birmingham in two weeks' time – actually just before your birthday.
Sounds great, when exactly?
12 March.
Could we get together and go out for a meal?
Maybe Mark could join us as well?
That's perfect.
Can you contact Mark and I'll book a restaurant?
Great! Anyway, better get back to work.
See you soon!
We can use actually to give more exact information, as in the letter.
We also use it a great deal in spoken English to say something which is surprising or different from what you expect, e.g. He looks Italian, but actually he's not.
Actually does not mean at the moment, e.g. The land is currently for sale.

tracks.title: Unit 100A - Letters or words?
sentences: Most abbreviations are spoken as individual letters.
EU, European Union.
UN, United Nations.
PM, Prime Minister.
MP, Member of Parliament.
BBC, British Broadcasting Corporation.
ID, identification.
Do you have an ID card?
PC, personal computer.
CV, curriculum vitae.
ISP, Internet Service Provider.
Occasionally abbreviations are spoken as words, e.g. AIDS and PIN.
We use the verb stand for to ask about the meaning of an abbreviation.
What does EU stand for?
European Union.

tracks.title: Unit 100B - Written forms only
sentences: Some abbreviations are written forms only, but pronounced as full words.
Mr Scott, mister Scott.
St Mark's Church, Saint Mark's Church.
Mrs Bryant, misses Bryant.
Dean St, Dean Street.
Dr Chapman, doctor Chapman.

tracks.title: Unit 100C - Abbreviations as part of the language
sentences: Some abbreviations from Latin are used as part of the language.
Et cetera, etc., and so on.
Exempli gratia, e.g., for example.
Id est, i.e., that's to say, or in other words.

tracks.title: Unit 100D - Shortened words
sentences: Some common English words can be shortened, especially in spoken English.
In some cases, the shorter form is more common and the full form sounds quite formal, e.g. refrigerator, influenza, gymnasium and veterinary surgeon.
Phone, telephone.
Fridge, refrigerator.
Maths, mathematics.
Exam, examination.
Board, whiteboard, smartboard, or blackboard.
Plane, aeroplane.
Case, suitcase.
Photo, photograph.
Ad, or advert, advertisement.
Flu, influenza.
Gym, gymnasium.
Lab, laboratory.
Bike, bicycle.
Sales rep, sales representative.
TV, or telly, television.
Vet, veterinary surgeon.
Paper, newspaper.
